title: Using port_handler=slipr4netns in pod prevents TCP connections
body:
/kind bug


**Description**

We want to build a POD with two containers, one of them a database and the other the application using this local database.  We also want to allow the application to see the source IP address for connections in order to enforce policy based on the requesting host.

The applications and DB can communicate within the POD and accept connections from outside hosts, but when we enable the port_handler=slirp4netns feature, the DB and application can no longer communicate within the POD and external requests are not routed to the application.

The attached script provides a test case that demonstrates the issue.  Use =gch-t info= for an explanation of the commands.

[gch-t.zip](https://github.com/containers/podman/files/8150025/gch-t.zip)

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. =gch-t image= to create OS image with ncat.

2. =gch-t -p launch= to run the image with ncat as the application.

3. =gch-t log= to watch the progress of ncat.  From another shell, use =netcat localhost 3000= to connect to the server in the pod.  It will report that there was a connection from 10.0.2.100.

4. =gch-t shutdown= to terminate the container.

5. =gch-t -px launch= to run the image with ncat as the application and the port_handler changed to slirp4netns.

6. =gch-t-log= to watch the progress of ncat.  From another shell, use =netcat localhost 3000= to connect to the server in the pod.  No connection is made.

The script also allows you to test ncat with and without the port_handler change and without a POD.  In both of these cases, the ncat operates correctly.


**Describe the results you received:**

Without a containing POD, the port_handler slirp4netns behaves as expected, forwarding the source IP to the application.

Within a containing POD, the port_handler slirp4netns appears to break connections to applications within containers in the POD.

**Describe the results you expected:**

We expect that the use of a POD and the port-handler would not be mutually exclusive.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version 3.4.4
```

We know that there is a newer version of podman.  It isn't yet available in Debian sid.

comments.body:
The default port_handler is rootlesskit, so I expect that, yes, it is fine in this case.

The example has only one /additional/ container in the pod.  With the sample script, there isn't a meaningful way to perform the test you suggest.

However, you can use the sample script to verify that, without a pod, the port_handler change exhibits the behavior as documented.  This is described in the script as well.
Unless you are creating a pod without an infra container, there are at least 2 containers in the pod, and the one you created is sharing a network namespace with the infra container.

I am corrected. 

I create only one container in the pod, so I don't know how I could perform the test you suggest.
Do you set --network on the container? If so this is incorrect, you should only use --network on the pod. If you also set it on the container you will overwrite the pod net namespace.
Excellent.  That seems to be the issue.  I guess I didn't try /all/ of the possible combinations.
